7.display: A circle of radius 3 m, centred on the post.

8.display: The perpendicular bisector of the line segment AB.

9.display: The angle bisector(s) of the angles formed where the lines cross.

13.display: A circle centred at P with radius 6 cm.

14.display: Every point on the perpendicular bisector is the same distance from A as from B; the only point on AB itself that satisfies this is the midpoint, so the bisector must cross AB there.

15.display: Never — it is always a straight line (the perpendicular bisector), never a curve or other shape.

16.display: No — the locus is a straight line exactly halfway between the two parallel lines, running parallel to both, not a single point.

18.display: It crosses AB at its midpoint (6 cm from each end), at 90°.

19.display: Anywhere along the angle bisector(s) of the angle(s) formed where the two paths cross.

20.display: A straight angle measures 180°, so bisecting it splits it into two equal angles of 90° each, which is a right angle.

21.display: A circle, radius 15 km, centred on the mast.

22.display: The perpendicular bisector of the line joining A and B.

24.display: A straight line running parallel to the coastline, 4 km out to sea.

25.display: 45° to each wall (along the angle bisector of the right angle).

26.display: No — the distance to each corner is √13 ≈ 3.6 m, which is further than the 3 m radius, so the water does not reach the corners.

27.display: Any point X on the perpendicular bisector forms two right-angled triangles with the midpoint of AB; both triangles have the same two side lengths (half of AB, and the shared perpendicular height), so by matching sides, XA must equal XB.

28.display: A ring shape (an annulus) between two circles centred on C — one with radius 5 cm and one with radius 8 cm.

29.display: Draw the perpendicular bisector of AB and keep the side closer to A; draw a circle of radius 20 km around B; the ship's positions are where both regions overlap.

30.display: Your own locus problem with two fixed points or a point and a line, with a correct description of where the locus lies.
